rendered_brief:
OTIF ROOT-CAUSE BOARD BRIEF

Total revenue at risk across 140 failed orders is AED 1,254,398.

RANKED DRIVERS BY CASH IMPACT

supplier — AED 570,948 (46% of failures, 56 orders)
Supplier lateness clustered across a wide range of inbound lead-time overruns, leaving insufficient stock at pick time. In the majority of clean supplier cases the late purchase-order quantity exceeded the shortfall, confirming that on-time delivery would have prevented the miss. Where demand also spiked, the supplier delay was still adjudicated dominant whenever timely receipt would have closed the gap. Recommended action: implement a tiered supplier scorecard with contractual on-time delivery penalties and a rolling safety-stock buffer sized to the observed lead-time variance; escalate the worst-performing suppliers — those with the longest overruns — to dual-source qualification within one planning cycle.

warehouse — AED 322,792 (26% of failures, 36 orders)
Two distinct warehouse failure modes were identified: dispatch delays (stock physically present but held beyond the promised release window) and true under-picks (stock on hand exceeded order quantity yet fewer units were shipped). Both modes are operationally controllable. Recommended action: introduce a same-day dispatch cut-off audit with automated escalation for any order not released within the agreed window, and deploy a pick-confirmation scan at the packing station to catch under-picks before despatch.

demand — AED 264,237 (21% of failures, 30 orders)
Forecast errors were consistently positive and large, with actual period demand substantially exceeding planned quantities across the affected orders. In several contested cases the forecast miss alone exceeded the shortfall, confirming demand under-forecasting as the binding cause even when a supplier delay co-occurred. Supplier lateness co-fired in roughly a third of demand-attributed orders, but was adjudicated secondary where the po quantity could not have covered the gap even if delivered on time. Recommended action: move the highest-volatility SKUs onto a shorter forecast review cycle and introduce a statistical safety-stock uplift calibrated to observed forecast-error distributions.

logistics — AED 96,421 (8% of failures, 18 orders)
Transit overages above lane SLA were the sole cause in the majority of logistics-attributed orders, with no dispatch delay or other domain firing. A subset involved both a warehouse dispatch delay and a transit overage; in those cases the larger of the two delays determined the dominant attribution. Supplier and demand signals were absent in all logistics-attributed orders, confirming this is a pure carrier-performance issue. Recommended action: renegotiate carrier SLAs on the highest-overage lanes and introduce real-time shipment tracking with automated customer notification when transit is projected to breach the promised date.

WHAT COULD NOT BE ESTABLISHED
The investigation could not determine whether supplier lateness was concentrated among a small number of vendors or spread broadly, as vendor-level identifiers were not available in the order signals. Similarly, the warehouse under-pick failure mode could not be linked to specific shift patterns, pick-zone configurations, or system errors without WMS transaction logs. For the demand-attributed orders, it was not possible to distinguish between a structural forecast-model deficiency and one-off promotional or event-driven demand spikes, as no promotional calendar or event flags were provided. Finally, in the contested dual-cause orders, the split of causal weight between demand and supplier could only be approximated from aggregate signals; a more precise attribution would require time-stamped stock-on-hand snapshots at the moment of pick.
